ocar.opportunity: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and X-ray microanalysis are foundational techniques for materials characterization and biological ultrastructure, but practitioners need comprehensive reference texts covering both instrumentation and analytical methods.
ocar.challenge: SEM and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) span physics, engineering, and biology, making a single authoritative reference spanning all relevant concepts difficult to compile and keep current as technology evolves.
ocar.action: This textbook systematically covers SEM instrumentation, electron-beam interactions with matter, secondary and backscattered electron imaging, X-ray microanalysis principles, and sample preparation for a range of material and biological specimens.
ocar.resolution: The text provides a rigorous yet accessible treatment of SEM and EDX methods, serving as the standard reference for practitioners in materials science, geology, and biological electron microscopy. It remains widely cited in connectomics and neuroscience EM studies for foundational methodology.
ocar.future_work: Continued updates are needed to address advances in field-emission SEM, cryo-SEM, and automated large-volume imaging pipelines that have transformed biological applications of SEM.

summaries.beginner: This is a textbook that explains how scanning electron microscopes work and how they are used to look at very small structures — from metals to biological cells. It describes both how to take images and how to identify what materials are present using X-rays.
summaries.intermediate: A comprehensive reference covering SEM instrumentation, electron-specimen interactions, imaging modes, and EDX microanalysis. It serves as a foundational methods reference for the electron microscopy community, including researchers using SEM-based approaches like FIB-SEM for connectomics.
summaries.advanced: This standard SEM/EDX reference text underpins a broad range of electron microscopy applications including FIB-SEM and SBEM used in connectomics. Its treatment of electron-beam physics, detector configurations, and sample preparation is foundational for understanding how large-volume biological imaging pipelines operate. The text predates many recent advances in automated biological SEM but remains the canonical starting point for practitioners.

discussion_prompts: What modifications to standard SEM sample preparation are needed when imaging biological tissue compared to materials science specimens?
How do the principles of secondary vs. backscattered electron contrast described in this text apply to the interpretation of FIB-SEM or SBEM images used in connectomics?
What are the physical limits on resolution in SEM imaging, and how have advances in electron sources (e.g., field-emission guns) pushed against these limits?
